sentences.text: I couldn't really say how long they continued, because he had to finish.
And we don't really-- we didn't have any calendar there.
We didn't know what time of the day it was, really, except when we knew that we had to go to the call-- roll call.
We didn't know what day-- date it is.
The experiments were on and off.
And he had so many, he had to do with a lot.
So he puts us aside, and we were just in waiting, you know?
Ladies in waiting, for-- probably for the right couple of twins that he would have had.
I think that he-- Mengele-- if, you know, when you-- when I think about Auschwitz, really, I remember only Mengele.
The name, Mengele and Auschwitz is just one.
And our status as being Mengele twins made us not feel different, but the others might have regarded us different because they knew that we won't be sent out to work.
We haven't been treated badly because we were supposed to be there.
We were his property.
So our status as Mengele twins might have been regarded by the others as different.
We definitely were not killed on the spot, or sent out of the camp.
Not by us.
Not by us, definitely not.
We didn't even know when Yom Kippur is.
I wouldn't have fasted, anyway.
[LAUGHS] We fasted enough as it was.
No, nothing at all.
Nothing at all.
Let's say, by tradition, I'm Jewish.
I feel that I am belonging to the Jewish race.
But I don't feel different.
And by religion, we haven't been brought up very religiously.
We knew that we are Jewish.
We have been taught the commandments, and we have been taught to be good human beings, to be a good example to the other races.
And that was our religion.
But we didn't keep Shabbat.
We didn't keep kosher.
Nothing.
And during the time in Auschwitz, I didn't even believe in religion.
I didn't believe that the God can look at this cruelty and at all these dreadful things.
I didn't believe in God.
Maybe it was just a shock to the-- I don't know.
I always-- maybe, when Annetta and I, when we talked together, we said, my God, something like that.
But that was just a matter of speaking.
We didn't-- or maybe we have prayed to God, but just by our ordinary words.
God, please, let us survive so we can see our mother and our sister and our-- I, my husband.
Let us survive.
And we said, God, please do it.
But then I haven't believed that the God is there, because when we found out that there was so much cruelty, and nobody helped-- nobody helped lift a finger.
If God would have wanted us to get out of there, he would have done something.
So I didn't believe in God.
But after the war, when I started a new life, I started to believe that there is some power over us.
And there must have been some reason for all this.
Don't say that I am very religious.
I'm liberal and I go to the synagogue.
I married a husband who made me feel that whatever we believe in is all right.
And he's a very, very understanding man.
You met him.
You know.
That was only because he had to stop.
The Allies came nearer to the Auschwitz camp, and he couldn't continue.
He had to pack up all what he had, and I don't know how he-- where did he disappear.
I don't know.
All of a sudden, we were told that the camp would be liquidated, and we were sent to Frauenlager.
That was women's lag-- women's camp.
And after that, soon after--
Yes.
We had been headed to the-- as a camp, we were taken there.
And we were not greeted gently because we have been the elite.
We have been the Mengele twins.
And the establishment in the men-- in the lager of the-- the camp of the women-- it was very harsh.
And the kapos were not very pleasant to us.
They didn't beat us, but they had-- didn't treat us very kindly.
Well, somebody has been standing in the wrong way, and she was kicked.
We were shouted at.
We were told that we are the-- we are the ones who never suffered so much as they did, or so.
You know.
It was the anger for the establishment to their own suffering.
They just shown their-- they-- they-- they just explain-- I don't know.
It was bursting out of them.
Look, they are prisoners who were treated better than we are.
And now they come, and they take our place in the bunk or something.
They want to stand in the queue to get the soup, and it's our turn to go there, or something.
But it was not long.
We were there just a couple of days, and then we were sent on a death march.
I don't think we thought about it very much.
We were quite expecting that this is the end, that maybe when we are sent-- when that camp is being liquidated-- that must mean that the war comes to the end.
And the camp we are pushed into, the other one, will be soon liquidated, too, and the end of the war will come.
That was our reaction.
We didn't look at it pessimistically.
We always tried to look at it from the better side, you see.
Well, there were disturbing informations.
Not many, really.
We have been told this was bombed, and Berlin was bombed and so on.
And war is ending and the Germans can't do very much.
And they will be, now, letting us out.
But we didn't really have any concrete news.
But then we were sent from there on the death march.
That was when we were lined up and, in four or more-- I don't know how many were we in a line.
And we have been marched out of Auschwitz.
And we thought that that will be the march to freedom.
But it wasn't.
It was a death march, which was really-- which was meant that people should die on the way along the road, as many as possible, because we haven't been given food.
And we had to march.
We couldn't rest.
On some occasions, we rested, too, when we were allowed.
We were sitting on the ground.
It was in January.
It was in-- that was winter.
There was snow.
And we have been allowed to stop.
And we sat, and we just rested in the sand-- in the-- in the snow.
I remember that instead of drinking water, we used the snow, just melt it in our hand and lap it.
Then at one stage, we have been-- we have been having some guards with dogs behind us.
And who couldn't continue walking and stopped, or even to relieve ourselves-- we had been very careful in how to relieve ourselves, because if you stopped out of the line, you got shot.
Or you were behind, and you might have frozen to death.
We have seen many dead bodies along the route.
We didn't know where we are.
Now we, the prisoners, some of them took the chance to take clothes off those corpses which were lying along the road, to make themselves more warm because it was very cold.
And we were-- Annetta and I were always together.
And we were two-- we had two other friends together, two younger girls, with whom we kept together.
And one time, we had to-- we could stop at some farm.
And there was a haystack, and we could sit around.
And somebody made a little fire.
I don't know if it was the guard.
The guard was probably in the-- in the farm.
I think we have been made-- given something to eat, but I don't remember details.
I remember only one dreadful thing.
Those beautiful shoes I talked about before, which were so wonderful.
My sister, unfortunately, didn't have those shoes-- the same kind-- but she had shoes.
I took them off, and they froze.
And they were smaller, and I couldn't put them on in the morning.
And I had to go without the shoes.
I had them with me, but I had to-- Annetta and friends helped me to put the straw with some rags, binding around-- binding around my feet that I could walk.
And eventually, the shoes defrosted, and I could put them on again.
But that was dreadful.
That's one part that I remember from that-- from that death march, apart of the dead bodies-- dead bodies that we have seen along the route.
And some of them died by bullet, and some of them died by just ex-- you know, they just died of hunger and being sick and frozen.
Then we have been taken to Ravensbruck.
We came, eventually, to Ravensbruck.
I don't know how long we did-- we travel because if I look at map, it's quite a long, long way.
And I don't remember that we would have been taken by train, but maybe-- maybe I have forgotten.
I don't know.
I couldn't really vouch for it.
We arrived at Ravensbruck, which was a Frau-- a women lager, camp where there were only women.
And again, we have been taken in a big hall, and-- always in group-- we have been given the chance to wash ourselves.
We have been given a bowl, a basin with water.
And we washed ourselves.
We were four girls.
First our faces, our hands, then a little bit down.
At the end, we used the same water for washing, maybe, maybe some of our clothes.
I don't know.
But at that time, I remember that we were lying in the big hall next to each other, and if somebody wants to turn-- had to nudge the other one to turn, too, because it was so little place.
We have been give some food there because I remember that we have been going out.
And we saw one old lady, probably old inmate.
She might not have been old.
She might have been 22.
She looked old.
She looked dreadful, a skeleton in rags.
And she was holding her portion of bread.
And then we have seen somebody coming and pushing her and taking the bread away.
We couldn't do anything about it.
That woman probably would have died, anyway.
And that other woman took it to survive.
It was like animals, trying to survive.
When you put food for two puppies, they push each other to eat more.
We took it like that.
We were very shocked by it.
We have seen it, and we were very upset about it, but we couldn't do anything about it.
And that was the way of life.
The strongest survived, and the ones who were lucky.
We considered ourselves, in a way, being lucky.
You know?
That's why we survived.
From there, after two days, I think, we were sent by kind of-- some kind of transport to a different place.
I think we were supposed to work in a munition factory, but it was bombarded or [INAUDIBLE].
I don't know.
It didn't work.
So from there, we were able to have showers because they had this place there.
And then we were sent to other camp, which was the last camp.
It was called Malhoff.
It was in Mecklenburg, in Germany.
If we can just go back to Ravensbruck--
Yes.
Many other nationalities.
I really could not say because we hadn't-- we didn't have any contact.
We have been kept together.
You know?
People who came from Czechoslovakia, from Theresienstadt, and were in Auschwitz, and were in that Familenlager, and were surviving-- they were always kept together.
And then from one place to the other, and as we kept together, the four girls, we didn't have contact with the others.
I know there were many other nationalities.
Hopeful, again, because we hoped that now we will be able to-- that is the end.
We always saw, behind the door, the-- the sunshine.
You know?
We always hoped.
For instance, once in-- I remember, in Auschwitz, when we have been out behind the barracks, and there was not much grass, just mud and so on.
And there was one-- it was a weed, but it was flowering, I think one of those yellow weeds.
I can't remember the name.
And we looked at it, and we were so happy to see something like that.
And we said to each other that, one day, we will have our garden.
One day we will be somewhere where there are flowers.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] What's your next question?
How did you [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] adapt to being in Ravensbruck after Auschwitz?
Did you feel--
We didn't have a chance.
We didn't have a chance to adapt there because we were sent away, straightaway.
But in the last camp, we have been more-- better handled.
We didn't have to work hard.
We have been even taken by an SS woman to somewhere in the fields, and there was some kind of-- I don't know.
We were having showers there.
And we had better food.
We had warm water.
We could wash our clothes.
Unfortunately, Annetta, at that time, had a typhoid, but not a very-- very strong one.
And there was a-- some kind of a doctor.
We didn't have medications, but Annetta survived.
And I didn't tell you of the death march, that we had a friend whom we supported, another one, apart of those two.
She couldn't walk.
She couldn't.
But we did get her up to the Ravensbruck.
And I don't know where did she go afterwards.
We were separated.
I don't know what happened to her.
But we were trying to help each other, you see.
I don't know.
I can't-- I can't say.
Hundreds.
But I don't know.
If I would estimate the number, I would tell you something.
I really don't know.
Yeah.
Malhoff.
Yes.
It was better.
We have been given more bread.
We have been given kind of a soup, which was more something in it.
I really don't remember exactly what.
But staying in that camp was just waiting for the end of the war, because we have been given some news, then, that it is getting to the end.
And we were hoping.
And the SS woman who was there was kinder to us, you know?
So we had the feeling that it won't take long.
How long were you there till liberation?
We came there, I think, in February.
And we were-- we left there in May.
Because-- beginning of-- no, it must have been March.
March-- because in May we were-- we met soldiers from the liberating army.
One night, we-- it was all quiet, and we were not called out.
And we found out that the guards are not there.
So we knew that we can do what we want.
We got some bread.
We got blankets.
And we got out from there.
I don't know how many people were there, but we mingled with the German population, because they were running away from their homes because the army was coming nearer.
And the guards left because the army was coming nearer.
But we have been told by somebody-- there were also people who were in-- Christian people, who were from Italy and other European countries who were [?
Laborwerks-- ?] labor camps.
And they told us, don't go this way.
They are coming, the Russians.
Go that way, they are the American.
And we have heard that the Russians were not so very gentle to young girls.
So we tried to run the other way, and fortunately.
After stopping in several houses of Germans where we helped ourselves to some food which was left there, or some clothes-- I even think I have left with a wallet with some German money.
And we took that, and we continued.
And we came where we met some German-- oh, sorry some-- some US Army.
And they were very good to us.
We were not alone.
There were many other people.
And they have established a little camp for us.
They had tents, and they gave us food.
That was the first time I have seen a negro, when he came-- a soldier-- into the tent to say hello to us.
We didn't speak English.
One of our girls-- one of our friends spoke a bit English, so she was the interpreter between the English, you know, the American soldiers and us.
And they give us cigarettes, blankets.
And we-- from then on, we are-- we knew that we are living.
And we have been trying to get to some points from where we could go back to Czechoslovakia, because everybody wanted to go home.
I mean, we four girls.
The other-- the other people on the-- on the way, the French who spoke English was from a very rich family.
Her father had, in Prague, a car-- was selling cars, and she knew a bit of driving.
So the American soldiers had there some jeep-- a German jeep, probably.
And they gave it to us, and she was going to drive us.
And we ended in a-- in a ditch.
Nothing happened to us.
Then some battalion got a-- a little carry-- how do you call it-- horse, one-drawn horse.
One little carriage for one horse.
And they got a horse, and they got the little carriage.
And they gave it.
We were with them.
I think one of them was one who was getting the horse to move.
And we were getting somewhere, again, to continue to get toward a point where we can go to-- to travel home.
And something happened to it, again.
I don't know.
But it was already quite a happy occasion.
We could laugh.
We could, you know.
There were Italians who killed a pig, and they roasted the pig on the road, and they gave us some-- something of it to eat.
We met other people.
We met, actually-- at night, we were sleeping in some haddock-- in some haystack.
And there were, mingled, all people together, not only survivors and the labor, but deserters from German army.
And there were two young boys who were dressed up in civilian clothing, but they must have been from the army.
And they were telling us they would like to come with us.
Could we help them to-- these guys, that they are some of, you know, our group.
And we couldn't, because they were well-fed, and they spoke only German.
And really, we didn't think that we could do something for them.
And we parted.
But that was-- you know, young people everywhere tried to survive.
Those German boys, we were sorry for them because they told to us they were in the army.
They couldn't do anything else but to go to the army.
And they didn't know anything about concentration camps.
When we told them something, they couldn't understand.
They didn't know anything.
And the German people, when we met them later, during-- we came to Lubeck.
Lubeck is a port in Germany.
How did we get there, I can't recollect.
Slowly.
And we were given money by somebody, probably some Unite-- international help or something.
We were given accommodation in some school, and we were given some money.
I went to a hairdresser to have my hair cut.
The hair dresser didn't even want to do it because she saw us, you know?
We were not really such good looking and clean looking.
We had washed and so on, but our hair didn't look so well.
But they did, and we paid for it with the money that we have been given by-- Did she actually say--
They didn't talk to us.
They did not know anything about what happened to the people.
They didn't know anything.
Then I had a toothache, and I had to go a tooth-- to a dentist.
He didn't know anything, and I had to pay him for this-- for service.
So people in Germany, well, they suffered themselves during the war, too.
And they were not very sympathetic because they didn't know anything.
I mean, that's what they told us.
Eventually, we got on a truck in Lubeck, which took us, slowly, to Czechoslovakia.
And we came to Prague-- Prague.
And we were looked after by Red Cross.
We were accommodated in a school.
We couldn't go to our home.
There was nothing.
We found out that there were Jewish Joint.
Jewish organizations started to try to find out through, I don't know, Red Cross archives or what, if somebody survived.
And we found out our family didn't come back.
They--
Well, we have been told later, from archives and from news, there have been-- my father died soon after they were in Lodz, in the ghetto.
He died.
And my mother was working in a straw factory.
They were making shoes-- covers for the shoes for the soldiers-- for German soldiers.
I don't know how much she suffered by it.
What my little sister did, I don't know.
I always hoped that she was [INAUDIBLE] when she was taken away.
That was in '41.
In '45, she would have been a teenager.
I hoped that she might have survived.
I hoped that one day we will see her, some day.
I remember we were walking through the streets of [INAUDIBLE].
[CRYING] There was a young girl, [SOBBING] and we called her-- called to her, Lizinka!
Because that was the nickname.
[CRYING] It was not her.
Well, eventually I found out, also, that my husband didn't come back.
There were-- there were survivors who told us that he didn't come back.
Where was he, and that he didn't survive.
Somebody told me he took his own life.
I don't know.
Somebody told me he threw himself on the-- on the electrified barb-- barbed wire.
I don't know.
He didn't come back.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We have found friends-- Christian friends-- who were very good to us, who took us in their house like a family.
We knew them.
They were friends from our family.
The husband was Ger-- was Jewish, and he was in Theresienstadt.
But his wife was not, and he was-- she was supporting him.
We called them mother and father.
But we were adults.
We were 21 already.
I was [INAUDIBLE].
And we have decided that we will do something with our lives.
So we enrolled ourselves into a board-- into a nursing school, which had a boarding-- it was a boarding school.
It was attached to a hospital run by nuns.
And we were taught by nuns.
And we were taught by doctors and nurses.
And we were there for our-- during our training.
And we had to work very hard in the hospital, and we had to study.
And we have been really different from the other girls.
None of them was Jewish.
They were all young Jewish-- girls, from the country, sometimes.
And when they were very upset that they have to be here without their mothers or something, we were telling them, don't be stupid.
But I remember that we didn't tell them much about our experiences because they were not interested.
When we came back, we found out that people didn't want to hear much.
They had their own worries during the Hitler regime, during the war.
They liked to be talking about their own problems, and didn't want to hear about others.
Or we didn't talk to them because we thought they wouldn't believe us.
Because it was so unbelievable, even to us, that we survived, that we went through it.
And then we didn't want to talk about it.
We thought-- just thought we will forget about everything.
And we studied.
And we-- we nursed.
And we had a lot of fun, also, because Annetta and I, we looked the same in the uniform, nurses' uniforms.
We looked the same, and people were-- were mixing us up.
That Annetta was working in one department, and when she finished, I went in there, and nobody knew that I am different.
And if I was told to do something, and I didn't know to, and I was told off, and then the matron was called to sort it out.
I was called Mrs.
Kunewalder, because the matron was very-- you know, she didn't call us by first name.
Mrs.
Kunewalder.
And we tried to-- to-- to live like young people.
Annetta, my sister, actually married a survivor who we met in Auschwitz when we were the twins, and on the twins barracks.
One twin died, and there was sent a couple of men with a stretcher from other camp to bring this corpse to the postmortem, or I don't know where.
And when these men came, we found out that we could talk to them.
We found out they are Czech.
And we spoke the Czech, a couple of pleasantries.
And when they went away, we found out that they were from-- from where they come or whatever.
And that was the last time-- first and last time, we saw them.
And after the war when we had our diplomas, and we went to celebrate, to dance, a gentleman came to my sister and said, would you dance with me?
I know you.
And she said, I don't recollect.
And he said, lift your sleeve.
If your number is similar to mine, we were together at the same time at the same-- and he recognized her.
And there was a romance after that, and she married him.
They were-- that was her husband who came with her to Australia and fathered her children.
My brother-in-law.
So we met there, in Auschwitz.
Now--
Well, it was shorter than it should have been.
It should have been for three years.
But after the war, they made everything shorter to have nurses.
So it was two and 1/2, probably.
And I was straightaway given a position as a nurse on a Czech hospital.
Annetta married, so she didn't work much, just short time.
And then we decided that we-- that was in '49, when-- '48 Israel was established, and we decided that we go to Israel.
So I was the first one to go to Israel.
And my sister and her husband and firstborn son came after me, and we were united again.
Well, I thought that that is where I belong.
I loved it.
I didn't know the language.
I didn't have a chance to learn the language because I had to work straightaway.
And I did get along.
I just made mistakes.
When I-- and a patient ask me, [HEBREW].
That means-- in Hebrew, it means, nurse, give me a bed pan.
And in Czech, syr mean a cheese.
And it was night, and I thought she's hungry.
So I went into the kitchen and brought her cheese.
And she said, I don't want cheese.
I want to have the bed pan.
[LAUGHTER] But I went along.
That was go-- all right.
I made friends.
And eventually, I-- as I was working in the hospital, I met a gentleman who was very nice.
And as Annetta was married and has-- lived in kibbutz-- Annetta and her husband lived in kibbutz because they couldn't establish outside without money.
I didn't want to be alone.
I felt that I have to start some-- some life of my own.
I married, but apart of him being such a nice person and loving me so much, we didn't understand each other.
He was religious, and I wasn't.
He was--
His name was Ziegfried Stiasny.
